name: Seneca the Younger
bio: A Stoic philosopher whose writings provide vital insight into daily life in Ancient Rome. He famously documented the noise, smells, and chaotic conditions of the public baths, highlighting that despite their advanced engineering, Roman public health still faced serious limitations.

name: Edwin Chadwick
bio: A Victorian social reformer whose 1842 report on the Sanitary Condition of the Labouring Population argued that disease was caused by bad air (miasma) and poor living conditions. His tireless campaigning eventually led to the 1848 Public Health Act.

name: Dr John Snow
bio: A pioneering Victorian physician who famously traced the source of the 1854 Broad Street cholera outbreak to a single contaminated public water pump. His work proved that cholera was a waterborne disease, fundamentally challenging the prevailing miasma theory.

name: Sir Joseph Bazalgette
bio: The brilliant Chief Engineer of London's Metropolitan Board of Works. Following the unbearable 'Great Stink' of 1858, he designed and constructed an extensive, revolutionary sewer network that fundamentally transformed London's sanitation and eliminated cholera.

name: Louis Pasteur
bio: A French chemist whose groundbreaking experiments in 1861 proved the Germ Theory of Disease. By demonstrating that microorganisms cause decay and illness, he finally discredited the miasma theory and revolutionized modern public health and sanitation.

name: King Henry VIII
bio: Despite presiding over a period where urban sanitation remained incredibly poor, Henry VIII established a strong royal navy. The loss of his flagship, the Mary Rose, near Southsea Castle provides vital historical evidence of Tudor life, showcasing the harsh conditions and diseases that sailors faced.

name: Robert Rawlinson
bio: A leading Victorian sanitary engineer and public health inspector. His devastating 1850 sanitary report on the horrific conditions in Portsmouth, written just after the 1849 cholera epidemic, played a pivotal role in exposing the fatal consequences of poor urban drainage and forcing local authorities to improve public health.

name: Simon Schama
bio: Sir Simon Schama is a leading contemporary British historian and author. He argues that Roman public health was driven as much by their desire to project 'Roman identity' and civilization as it was by a genuine desire to stop disease.
